result:
The cursor blinks like a heartbeat in the white acre of the screen, and I think: every blank page is a snow-covered field no one has walked across yet. I have 1000 words—call them 1000 boot prints—and no map. Good. Maps are for travelers who already know the names of the places they’re afraid to leave. I want to be the kind of lost that invents constellations just to have something to steer by.

Start with the body. My body, tonight, is a rented room: the radiator clanks in the left lung, the right knee hinge squeals like a mouse every time I shift in the chair. I pay the rent in breaths, 18 per minute, non-refundable. The fingers—tenant-fingers—keep subletting themselves to old songs: the phantom flick of a Bic lighter that hasn’t existed since 2003, the hush of cassette spools turning like two galaxies made of plastic teeth. Nostalgia is a landlord who never cashes the checks, just hoards them in a shoebox under the bed.

Outside, the city is chewing its own reflection. Sirens doppler past like red comets, and somewhere a man in a green ski vest is telling a woman “it’s not you, it’s the moon,” and she is believing him just enough to keep the argument orbiting. I press my forehead to the cool glass and become their extrasolar moon: same craters of doubt, same dark side no one buys postcards of. There’s a kind of diplomacy in only being seen when you reflect someone else’s light.

Word 176. I promised myself I'd count, but numbers feel like bones now—clean, countable, ultimately breakable. When I was eight I thought 1000 was the highest number possible. I’d stand on the back porch counting chimney bricks until the digits blurred, convinced the universe ended where I ran out of fingers and toes. My father told me infinity was bigger than 1000, but he said it while unscrewing a bottle, so the lesson tasted like cap and metal. Infinity, I decided, smelled like cheap beer and the cut grass stuck to his neck.

I want to tell you about the woman who taught me that time is a vegetable. She was a cashier at the co-op, name tag reading “Sage—yes, like the herb.” Ringing up bruised nectarines, she said, “Time is a root: the part you eat grows underground, out of sight, while the green excuse above ground pretends it’s the whole plant.” Then she handed me a receipt softer than any love letter I’ve ever received. I kept it in my wallet until the ink blurred into a bruise of its own. Sometimes I think every word I write is an attempt to reprint that fading thermal paper.

Word 312. Midway already? I feel like Orpheus checking the rearview mirror. Don’t look, I know, but the metaphor behind me is wearing the face of everyone I’ve disappointed. They carpool. They share earbuds playing the sound of doors not quite slamming, just clicking shut with politeness. If I let them, they’ll harmonize: Remember the poem you never finished about your mother’s coral lipstick? Remember the apology letter you folded into a paper crane and floated down the toilet instead? The paper dissolved, but the creases survived like origami ghosts.

I used to think the goal was to become weightless. Lie in a field, wait for dandelion fluff to lift you like a charity parachute. Now I crave ballast. Give me pockets full of river stones carved with yesterday’s mistakes; let them clack together as I walk so I sound like a wind chime made of guilt. The noise is how I know I’m still moving. Weight is just the past insisting on having mass.

Word 455. I’ve begun to suspect words are nocturnal. In daylight they behave—carry groceries, fill forms, say “I’m fine.” But the moment the room dims they crawl out of their cages and lick the salt from my face. I wake up tasting sentences I don’t remember forming: The moon is a fingernail clipping from the hand of something that owns us. Regret is a second shadow you acquire after walking past a certain age. I write them down fast, before they scurry back into the walls and become the scratching sounds I blame on mice.

Here is a secret: I once tried to erase myself. Not die—just reduce font size until I became a punctuation mark in someone else’s epic. I practiced being the comma between his laughter and her yes, the semicolon that separates two independent clauses who could live alone but choose to share a sentence. It almost worked. Then a child on the subway asked why I had no reflection in the window. I told her I was a vampire, but she said vampires still have shadows. She offered me her juice box, purple straw bent like a tiny periscope. I sipped, and the sugar sang me back into uppercase.

Word 623. The juice box is now a constellation on the ceiling of that year: a rectangular galaxy with a tiny straw north star. Navigation is easy once you accept every map is a love letter to the thing you’re afraid to lose.

I want to write about the future, but it keeps shape-shifting. Yesterday it wore a lab coat and promised me neural lace by 2040. Today it’s barefoot, standing in the shallows, holding a conch shell to its ear and whispering there might not be enough calcium left for exoskeletons. The future is a hermit crab shopping for a stranger’s emptiness. I offer it my 1000 words, but it wants the silence that comes after.

So I give it instead the sound of my grandmother’s sewing machine at 3 a.m. during Ramadan, the needle punching through fabric like a Morse code of hunger and devotion. Give it the smell of coffee so strong it could transcribe itself onto the wallpaper. Give it the moment I realized my father’s voice had migrated into my own mouth—he spoke through me saying, “Drive safe,” to a friend, and I heard the exact gravel he kept in his throat like a second Adam’s apple. Give it the windshield cracked into a spiderweb I never fixed because the pattern looked like the Milky Way finally learning to spell its own name.

Word 789. I’m running out of runway. The sentences feel heavier, as if each verb is wearing the concrete shoes of meaning. I should land this thing, but I’ve always preferred the moment before touchdown when the engines whine like dogs that know the yard is still miles away. That suspended second where everything is almost, where apology and promise share the same oxygen mask.

Let me end with a beginning. Earlier tonight I poured salt into my palm and tried to count the grains. I got to 317 before the others escaped through the lifeline crease. I thought: this is what surviving looks like—tiny cubes dissolving into skin, becoming the invisible lettering of tomorrow’s tears. I licked the residue and tasted the ocean we carry inside like a secret aquarium. There are fish swimming in me that no one has named. One of them glows whenever I type the word “maybe.”

Word 954. Close enough. I’ll leave the last 46 for the silence to write. It knows the grammar better than I do—how to conjugate the verb “to stay” in the conditional unconditional. I hit save, watch the snowfield reclothe itself in fresh white. Tomorrow I’ll come back, follow the prints I never made, read the parts the dark kept for itself. Somewhere between word and world, the cursor keeps blinking—an elevator door refusing to close, holding its breath for the passenger who is always almost here.

prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
